requirements: The core contributions of LLMServingSim 2.0 have been reproduced: a unified, runtime-driven simulator for heterogeneous and disaggregated LLM serving infrastructures that accurately reproduces throughput, latency (TTFT/TPOT/ITL), memory, prefix hit rate, and power metrics across diverse hardware (RTX A6000, H100, TPU-v6e-1, PIM) and serving techniques (multi-instance dense, prefix caching, PD disaggregation, MoE).

sub_tasks.requirements: Environment, datasets, and models required to run LLMServingSim 2.0 have been prepared.

The simulator's build environment is set up correctly: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, Python 3.10.12, gcc/g++ 11.4.0 or higher, x86-64 architecture, and the ASTRA-Sim/Chakra prerequisites (matching the prebuilt Docker image).

verification.spec: Verify the runtime environment matches the documented prerequisites: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (`lsb_release -a`), Python 3.10.12 (`python3 --version`), gcc/g++ >= 11.4.0 (`gcc --version`, `g++ --version`), x86_64 architecture (`uname -m`), and presence of ASTRA-Sim/Chakra build dependencies (cmake, protobuf, boost, etc.). Optionally check that the prebuilt Docker image referenced in README is pullable.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to clone the LLMServingSim repository with all submodules (including the modified astra-sim and Chakra) and to build the submodules via the provided compile.sh script.

verification.spec: Clone the LLMServingSim repository recursively (`git clone --recursive`) ensuring astra-sim and Chakra submodules are present, then run the provided `compile.sh` script. Verify that the script terminates with exit code 0 and produces the expected built artifacts (astra-sim binary and Chakra Python bindings).

sub_tasks.requirements: The ShareGPT dataset is available for use as the workload source for trace generation.

verification.spec: Verify that the ShareGPT dataset (e.g., ShareGPT_V3_unfiltered_cleaned_split.json) is downloadable and accessible at the path expected by dataset/sharegpt_parser.py, and that loading it yields a non-empty list of conversation records with the expected schema (conversations field with from/value entries).

sub_tasks.requirements: Workload trace JSONL files are produced with fields `input_toks`, `output_toks`, `arrival_time_ns`, and `input_tok_ids`, using a Poisson arrival process at 10 requests/sec sampling 300 requests from ShareGPT (matching dataset/sharegpt_parser.py).

verification.spec: Invoke dataset/sharegpt_parser.py (or its equivalent) with Poisson arrival rate 10 req/s and 300 sampled requests. Load the resulting JSONL and verify: (a) exactly 300 records, (b) each record has keys `input_toks`, `output_toks`, `arrival_time_ns`, `input_tok_ids`, (c) arrival_time_ns is monotonically non-decreasing, (d) inter-arrival times approximate an exponential distribution with mean ~1e8 ns (Kolmogorov–Smirnov p>0.05 or mean within 10% of 1e8), (e) len(input_tok_ids) == input_toks.

sub_tasks.requirements: The model architectures used in evaluation are available as HuggingFace-compatible configs: Llama 3.1-8B, Llama 3.1-70B, Phi-mini MoE, and Mixtral 8x7B.

verification.spec: Verify HuggingFace configs for `meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B`, `meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B`, `microsoft/Phi-mini-MoE-instruct` (or equivalent Phi-mini MoE), and `mistralai/Mixtral-8x7B-v0.1` are loadable via `AutoConfig.from_pretrained` and that their hidden_size, num_hidden_layers, num_attention_heads, and (for MoE) num_local_experts match the paper's evaluation setup.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Operator-level Profiler that produces hardware performance profiles (latency and power per operator) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented for a PyTorch/HuggingFace-based profiling API that records per-operator latency for a single decode block per model–device pair on real hardware, completing in roughly 2.1 hours for Llama 3.1-70B on H100.

verification.spec: Run the Operator-level Profiler on Llama 3.1-70B targeting a single H100 GPU for one decode block and measure end-to-end wall-clock time of the profiling pass. Verify it completes within tolerance of the reported ~2.1 hours.

sub_tasks.requirements: The profiler captures per-operator power measurements alongside latency, producing power profiles used by the power model.

verification.spec: Run the Operator-level Profiler and confirm the emitted operator profile records both latency and power for each operator (e.g., via nvidia-smi/NVML sampling concurrent with kernel execution). Verify the resulting profile JSON/CSV contains non-zero `power_W` (or equivalent) fields per operator, and that the power model later consumes these values.

sub_tasks.requirements: The profiler supports GPU backends (RTX A6000, H100) using the same profiling API with vLLM as the serving framework.

verification.spec: Run the profiler API against an H100 GPU using vLLM as the serving framework on Llama 3.1-8B; verify it emits an operator-level latency profile JSON consumable by the simulator. Then re-run the same API path against an RTX A6000 target spec (or skip with --device flag) and verify the same code path executes without GPU-specific branching.

sub_tasks.requirements: The profiler is extended to a TPU-v6e-1 backend producing TPU-specific operator profiles that can be ingested by the simulator.

verification.spec: Invoke the operator profiler with a TPU-v6e-1 backend on a small model (e.g., Llama 3.1-8B single decode block) and verify it produces a TPU-specific operator profile file containing per-operator latency entries in a schema accepted by the simulator's profile loader (load the profile and run a short simulation without error).

sub_tasks.requirements: The simulator can ingest operator-level profiles from external hardware simulators (e.g. PIM simulators) without requiring physical hardware.

verification.spec: Provide a synthetic operator-profile file in the PIM profile format (no real hardware) and run main.py with a PIM-enabled cluster config. Confirm the simulator ingests the external profile, maps PIM operators using its latencies, and produces simulation output without invoking real hardware.

sub_tasks.requirements: Collected profiles are stored in a reusable format (e.g. under profiler/) so they can be reused across experiments without re-running hardware profiling.

verification.spec: Run the profiler once on a small model and confirm it writes a reusable profile artifact (e.g. JSON/CSV) under profiler/ (or analogous directory). Then run main.py twice consecutively pointing at the same profile path and confirm the second run does not re-invoke profiling (e.g. by checking timestamps / absence of GPU profiling logs).

sub_tasks.requirements: The simulator input/output interface and configuration schema described in Section IV-A and Appendix G are implemented.

Code parses cluster configuration JSON files (cluster_config/{name}.json) containing fields `num_nodes`, `link_bw`, `link_latency`, `num_instances`, `cpu_mem`, `model_name`, `hardware`, `npu_mem`, `npu_num`, `npu_group`, and `pd_type`, plus optional `placement`, `pim_config`, `power`, and `cxl_mem`.

verification.spec: Load a representative cluster_config/{name}.json file with the config parser and assert that all required keys parse without error: num_nodes, link_bw, link_latency, num_instances, cpu_mem, model_name, hardware, npu_mem, npu_num, npu_group, pd_type. Then load an extended config containing optional keys placement, pim_config, power, cxl_mem and assert they are accepted and reflected in the constructed cluster object.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code parses workload trace JSONL files (dataset/{name}.jsonl) where each line is a request containing `input_toks`, `output_toks`, `arrival_time_ns`, and `input_tok_ids`.

verification.spec: Create a small dataset/test.jsonl file with several lines, each containing input_toks, output_toks, arrival_time_ns, input_tok_ids. Invoke the workload loader and assert each parsed request exposes those four fields with correct types (ints / list[int]).

sub_tasks.requirements: The Execution Planner that performs one-time initialization of the Serving Engine has been implemented.
The Execution Planner takes workload and cluster configurations and configures the Request Router to dispatch runtime requests to the appropriate MSGs.

verification.spec: Instantiate the ExecutionPlanner with a workload + a cluster config containing 2 MSGs (different models). Confirm the constructed Request Router exposes a mapping/dispatch method that, given a request with a target model name, routes it to the MSG serving that model. Verify by enqueuing two requests for different models and checking each lands in the correct MSG's queue.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Execution Planner instantiates one Model Serving Group (MSG) per model, assigns devices to form a customizable device pool, and installs serving policies including parallelism strategies, compute and memory offloading rules, KV cache management, and memory sharing.

verification.spec: Construct an ExecutionPlanner with a cluster config defining two distinct models and policies (different TP degrees, offloading rules, KV management). After planner.initialize(), assert the Serving Engine contains exactly one MSG per model, each MSG.device_pool contains the configured devices, and each MSG's installed policy attributes reflect the configured parallelism, offloading, KV cache management, and memory sharing settings.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Execution Planner initializes the System Simulator with cluster-level timing, network, memory, and topology configurations.

verification.spec: After ExecutionPlanner initialization with a cluster config specifying link_bw, link_latency, memory tiers, and topology, assert that the System Simulator (ASTRA-sim/Chakra-based) object exposes those parameters: topology graph nodes/edges match num_nodes, link bandwidth/latency fields equal the configured values, and memory-tier objects exist matching the cluster spec.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Model Serving Group (MSG) and its internal components have been implemented.
Each MSG maintains a request queue that receives requests from the Request Router and tracks per-request statistics (queueing delay, TTFT, TPOT, end-to-end latency) until completion.

verification.spec: Construct an MSG, push 3 synthetic requests with known arrival times into it via the Request Router, run a short simulation. Assert each completed request record contains numeric, non-negative queueing_delay, TTFT, TPOT, and end-to-end latency, and that TTFT >= queueing_delay and end_to_end >= TTFT.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Batch Scheduler within an MSG selects pending requests, evaluates system/device memory capacity, KV cache footprint and configured max batch size, and consults the memory model (including prefix-cache residency and eviction cost) before forwarding the batch to the operation mapper. The batching behavior mirrors vLLM's continuous batching scheduler.

verification.spec: Configure an MSG with max_batch=4 and a small KV memory budget. Submit 8 requests whose total KV footprint exceeds available memory. Assert: (a) formed batches never exceed max_batch; (b) when memory_model.fits(batch) returns False, the batch is not forwarded to the operation mapper (verified via mock/spy or log); (c) prefix-cache residency and eviction-cost checks are invoked during batch formation.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Operation Mapper assigns operators to devices within the MSG based on configured parallelism strategies and optional compute/memory offloading rules, attaches per-operator latency and power estimates from the profile, and supports fine-grained operator-level offloading (e.g. attention to PIM or experts/KV caches to specific memory devices).

verification.spec: Build an MSG with a heterogeneous device pool (GPU + PIM) and TP policy plus an offloading rule mapping attention to PIM. Provide a batch and run the operation mapper. Assert: each operator in the produced mapping has a `device` field (attention->PIM, others->GPU), and `latency`/`power` fields populated from the profile. Verify operator placement matches the configured TP/offloading policy.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Operation Scheduler constructs an execution DAG that encodes data dependencies, ordering constraints, parallelism strategies, and required communication/memory operations, and emits the resulting execution graph to the System Simulator.

verification.spec: Feed the operation scheduler a set of mapped operators with known dependencies and a TP parallelism policy. Assert the produced execution graph is a DAG (no cycles via topological sort), contains nodes for the original operators plus inserted communication/memory ops (e.g. all-reduce for TP, KV load/store), and that data-dependency edges are preserved. Confirm the graph object is the artifact passed to System Simulator.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Memory Model governing multi-tier KV cache management and device memory usage has been implemented.
PagedAttention-style block-based KV cache allocation is implemented with a configurable block size (default 16, also supports 256 for LMCache CPU caching).

verification.spec: Inspect the memory model implementation: confirm a configurable `block_size` parameter is exposed via the `--block-size` CLI flag in main.py and propagated to the KV cache allocator. Run two short simulations with --block-size 16 and --block-size 256 and verify both complete and produce different KV-block-count statistics.

sub_tasks.requirements: KV cache eviction and promotion decisions are derived from per-device memory capacity and cache residency, with the corresponding load and store overheads injected into the execution graph.

verification.spec: Configure an MSG with a small device memory and submit a request stream that forces KV eviction. Inspect the resulting execution graph and assert it contains explicit KV `store` operations when KV blocks are evicted from device, and `load` operations when promoted back, with correct sizes derived from per-device capacity and residency state.

sub_tasks.requirements: The memory hierarchy supports four explicit tiers — device memory (HBM), host (CPU) DRAM, CXL-attached memory, and storage — each with configurable capacity, latency, and bandwidth.

verification.spec: Load a cluster config that defines all four tiers (HBM, CPU DRAM, CXL memory, storage) with distinct capacity/latency/bandwidth. Assert the memory model exposes exactly these four tier types as first-class objects and that each tier stores configured capacity, latency, and bandwidth values returned by API queries.

sub_tasks.requirements: Prefix KV cache is represented via a radix-tree structure (analogous to SGLang) at each enabled tier, instantiated per-MSG when prefix caching is device-only, shared per-node when CPU is used, and globally shared when a CXL memory pool is enabled.

verification.spec: Run three configurations of the prefix cache: (1) device-only -> assert each MSG owns a distinct radix-tree instance; (2) CPU shared -> assert MSGs colocated on the same node share a single CPU radix-tree object while keeping per-MSG device caches; (3) CXL pool -> assert all MSGs in the cluster share a single global radix-tree object. Confirm the radix-tree class is used (SGLang-style).
verification.paper_cite: Section V.B (Prefix caching and multi-MSG sharing)

sub_tasks.requirements: On a prefix hit, the operation mapper reduces effective execution latency, and on a miss requiring a non-device tier, the operation scheduler automatically inserts layer-wise KV transfer operations from the appropriate memory tier whose cost is evaluated by the System Simulator.

verification.spec: Send two identical-prefix requests through an MSG with CPU-tier prefix caching enabled. For the second request, assert (a) the operation mapper reports reduced effective prefill latency vs. the first (cache hit), and (b) the execution graph contains layer-wise KV transfer operations sourced from the CPU tier whose cost is reflected in the System Simulator's reported runtime.

sub_tasks.requirements: Memory sharing across MSGs (cluster-wide effects, e.g. centralized CPU/CXL prefix cache pools) is explicitly modeled.

verification.spec: Run a 2-MSG simulation sharing a CPU prefix cache pool. Issue overlapping requests so MSG-A inserts a prefix and MSG-B then queries the same prefix. Assert MSG-B observes a hit served from the shared CPU pool (verified by hit-rate stats and a KV transfer op from CPU tier in B's graph), demonstrating cluster-wide memory sharing across MSGs.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Power Model has been implemented as described in Section IV-C.

Per-MSG instantaneous power is decomposed into seven components: accelerators, CPUs, DRAM, interconnect links (including switches), NICs, storage devices, and a remaining `others` category (motherboard, cooling, etc.).

verification.spec: Run a short simulation with power modeling enabled and inspect the output energy breakdown. Verify the report contains exactly seven named components: accelerators, CPUs, DRAM, interconnect links (incl. switches), NICs, storage, and others. Confirm in source that the power model class enumerates these seven categories.

sub_tasks.requirements: Accelerator power follows a three-state model (idle, active, standby) that captures utilization-dependent behavior.

verification.spec: Construct a workload with alternating active and idle pulses (e.g., 10 requests every 60s as in the paper). Run the simulator with power modeling on a GPU configuration and inspect the per-timestep accelerator power log. Verify three distinct power levels are observed (idle baseline, active peak, standby intermediate) corresponding to the three-state model.

sub_tasks.requirements: DRAM and interconnect link energy is modeled proportionally to the volume of data transferred.

verification.spec: Construct two synthetic workloads that differ only in volume of DRAM/interconnect bytes moved (e.g., double the KV transfer size). Run the simulator and verify DRAM and link energy components scale approximately linearly (≈2x) with transferred bytes, while constant-power components do not.

sub_tasks.requirements: CPUs, NICs, storage devices, and the `others` category are modeled with constant per-component power values (with `others` including any unused/idle GPUs in a multi-GPU server).

verification.spec: Run two simulations of different durations and workload intensities with the same hardware config. Verify that CPU, NIC, storage, and 'others' instantaneous power values remain constant over time (independent of utilization), and that on a 4-GPU server with TP=1, the 'others' bucket reflects 3 idle GPUs as constant power (matches Fig. 6(c) observation).

sub_tasks.requirements: The simulator aggregates per-MSG instantaneous power into a system-level instantaneous power trace and total system energy consumption.

verification.spec: Run a multi-MSG simulation and verify the simulator outputs (a) a system-level instantaneous power trace over time and (b) a total energy value, and that the system-level power at each timestep approximately equals the sum of per-MSG instantaneous power plus shared/system constants.
verification.paper_cite: Section IV.C (Power model) and Section IV.A (Outputs)

sub_tasks.requirements: The System Simulator (the network and execution backend extending ASTRA-sim and Chakra) has been implemented.
The System Simulator consumes operator-annotated execution DAGs (with device type, latency, memory, communication, and power info) emitted by MSGs and evaluates end-to-end cluster execution.

verification.spec: Inspect the interface between MSG.operation_scheduler and System Simulator. Verify each emitted graph node carries attributes for device type, latency, memory, communication info, and power info, and that edges encode data dependencies. Run a small simulation and dump one graph to confirm.

sub_tasks.requirements: ASTRA-sim has been extended (under astra-sim/) to support heterogeneous compute fabrics and operator-driven inference execution graphs rather than only repetitive training collectives.

verification.spec: Inspect the astra-sim/ subtree in the repository and verify it is a modified ASTRA-sim fork with added support for heterogeneous device types (multiple device classes in topology config) and operator-driven inference graphs. Check that test configs in the repo include heterogeneous device pools (e.g., GPU+PIM) consumable by astra-sim.

sub_tasks.requirements: Chakra has been extended to express inference-specific parallelism (tensor, pipeline, data, and expert parallelism) within execution traces.

verification.spec: Inspect the Chakra extensions in the repository and verify execution-trace generation code emits node/edge types specific to inference parallelism: tensor, pipeline, data, and expert parallelism (e.g., all-to-all comm nodes for EP, layer-stage edges for PP). Run a small MoE example and verify EP-specific nodes appear in the trace.

sub_tasks.requirements: The System Simulator models synchronization overhead, network contention, inter-device communication latency, and bandwidth contention based on cluster topology and configuration.

verification.spec: Run two simulations with identical workload but different cluster topologies (e.g., NVLink vs PCIe link_bw in cluster_config) and verify the reported inter-device communication latency and end-to-end TTFT differ in the expected direction (lower bandwidth -> higher latency). Also verify that increasing concurrent requests produces measurable bandwidth contention effects (per-op transfer latency grows with concurrency).

sub_tasks.requirements: PIM operations beyond memory load/store primitives are added so that PIM-accelerated execution can be simulated at the system level.

verification.spec: Run a GPU+PIM configuration (256 channels HBM2 @ 2000 MT/s per Section VII.C) with attention offloading enabled. Verify that the execution graph contains PIM-specific compute operations (e.g., PIM-GEMV for attention) beyond simple load/store, and that the System Simulator produces non-zero PIM compute latency for these operations.
verification.paper_cite: Section IV.D (System Simulator) and Section VII.C

sub_tasks.requirements: The runtime-driven Serving Engine loop coordinating the Request Router, MSGs, and System Simulator has been implemented.
The Request Router supports configurable request-routing policies via `--request-routing-policy` (e.g. round-robin, load-balanced) across MSGs.

verification.spec: Launch the simulator with --request-routing-policy=round-robin across 2 MSGs and verify request assignments alternate. Then launch with --request-routing-policy=load-balanced and verify the simulator assigns more requests to the less-loaded MSG over time.

sub_tasks.requirements: MSG-level serving techniques described in Section V-A have been implemented.
Heterogeneous device pools within a single MSG are supported, with operator placement decided at operator granularity by the operation mapper based on installed policies.

verification.spec: Configure a single MSG with a heterogeneous device pool (e.g., 1xH100 + 1xPIM) and an offloading policy. Run a simulation and verify the operation mapper assigns different operators to different device types within the same MSG (inspect resulting graph for mixed device-type annotations on operators).

sub_tasks.requirements: Attention offloading to PIM is supported: when enabled (`--enable-attn-offloading`), attention operators are mapped to PIM while remaining operators run on GPU, and required activation/KV transfers between GPU and PIM are automatically inserted by the operation scheduler.

verification.spec: Run main.py with a GPU+PIM cluster config and `--enable-attn-offloading`. Inspect the generated execution graph/log and verify: (a) attention operators are assigned to PIM devices, (b) non-attention operators remain on GPU, (c) the operation scheduler automatically inserts data-movement nodes transferring activations/KV between GPU and PIM before and after attention. Compare to a run without the flag where attention stays on GPU.
verification.paper_cite: Section V.A (Heterogeneous devices and operator-granular offloading); Fig. 3(a)

sub_tasks.requirements: Sub-batch interleaving (SBI), as proposed in NeuPIMs, is supported via `--enable-sub-batch-interleaving` to split batches between GPU compute and PIM attention.

verification.spec: Run the GPU+PIM configuration twice on a workload with batch size >= 256: once with `--enable-sub-batch-interleaving` and once without. Verify the SBI run produces overlapping GPU-compute and PIM-attention scheduling in the execution graph (sub-batches interleaved) and that throughput improves over the non-SBI GPU+PIM run when sustained large batches are present, replicating the trend in Fig. 10(a).

sub_tasks.requirements: MoE execution is supported within a single MSG, including configurable expert placement across devices, expert offloading to host memory, and per-token expert routing.

verification.spec: Configure a Mixtral-8x7B MSG with 4 GPUs and EP=4 (per Fig. 3(b)). Run the simulator and verify (a) experts are placed across devices per the placement policy, (b) per-token expert routing decisions are made at each MoE layer, and (c) when expert offloading is enabled, evicted experts are loaded from host memory with corresponding load ops in the execution graph.

sub_tasks.requirements: A configurable Expert Router supports multiple routing schemes selected via `--expert-routing-policy`, including random selection, round-robin, proportional-load balancing, and user-defined policies.

verification.spec: Run an MoE simulation with each of --expert-routing-policy in {random, round-robin, proportional-load}. Verify routing distributions differ as expected: round-robin yields near-uniform expert assignment per token order; proportional-load balances assignments according to current load; random produces a uniform random distribution. Also verify a user-defined Python callable can be plugged in as a policy.

sub_tasks.requirements: Expert parallelism (EP) is supported, and the operation scheduler automatically inserts expert loading and cross-device all-to-all communication operations into the execution graph.

verification.spec: Run a Mixtral-8x7B EP=4 simulation and inspect the execution graph: confirm the operation scheduler automatically inserts (a) expert-loading operations when experts are not resident on the assigned device, and (b) all-to-all communication operations between devices around MoE layers, without explicit user specification.

sub_tasks.requirements: Tensor parallelism (TP), pipeline parallelism (PP), and data parallelism (DP) are supported, configurable per MSG via cluster_config fields such as `npu_num` and `npu_group`.

verification.spec: Run three simulations of Llama 3.1-70B configured for (a) TP=4, (b) PP=4, and (c) DP=4 via cluster_config fields npu_num and npu_group. Verify the resulting execution graphs contain (a) all-reduce ops within layers for TP, (b) pipeline stage boundaries for PP, and (c) independent per-replica request streams for DP.

sub_tasks.requirements: System-level serving techniques described in Section V-B have been implemented.

Prefill–Decode (PD) disaggregation is supported: the Execution Planner instantiates separate prefill and decode MSGs for the same model with arbitrary M:N mapping, configurable via cluster_config `pd_type`.

verification.spec: Create a cluster_config JSON with `pd_type` specifying separate prefill and decode MSGs at e.g. a 2:4 (M:N) ratio for the same model. Run main.py and verify: (a) the Execution Planner instantiates the requested number of prefill and decode MSGs, (b) prefill MSGs schedule layer-wise KV transfer operations to the assigned decode MSG, (c) requests flow from prefill to decode and complete. Repeat with a different M:N ratio (e.g., 1:1) and verify the planner adapts.

sub_tasks.requirements: Prefill MSGs automatically insert layer-wise KV cache transfer operations to their associated decode MSG via the memory model, with inter-node communication latency evaluated by the System Simulator according to the configured network topology and bandwidth.

verification.spec: In a PD-disaggregated simulation, inspect the prefill MSG's emitted execution graph and verify layer-wise KV cache transfer operations are automatically inserted from prefill to decode MSG. Then run with two different inter-node bandwidth configurations and verify the KV-transfer latency reported by the System Simulator scales accordingly.

sub_tasks.requirements: Prefill and decode MSGs may use heterogeneous hardware compositions (different accelerators, memory hierarchies, interconnects).

verification.spec: Configure a PD-disaggregated deployment with prefill MSGs on H100 GPUs and decode MSGs on A100 GPUs (different memory hierarchies and interconnects), as illustrated in Fig. 4(a). Run the simulator and verify it completes successfully, that prefill and decode operators use their respective device profiles, and that inter-MSG KV transfer uses the configured cross-hardware link.

sub_tasks.requirements: Prefix caching at the device tier is implemented (`--enable-prefix-caching`), mirroring vLLM's native prefix caching with block size 16.

verification.spec: Run a single-MSG simulation with --enable-prefix-caching on workloads with shared prefixes (sampled from ShareGPT). Verify (a) the device-tier prefix cache uses block size 16, (b) prefix hit rate increases over time as requests accumulate, and (c) hits reduce TTFT compared to a baseline run with prefix caching disabled.
verification.paper_cite: Section V.B (Prefix caching) and Section VII.A (Memory usage)

sub_tasks.requirements: Multi-MSG prefix cache sharing is implemented via `--enable-prefix-sharing` and `--prefix-storage`, supporting per-node CPU-resident shared cache (LMCache-style, block size 256) and globally shared CXL memory pool prefix caches.

verification.spec: Run main.py with `--enable-prefix-sharing` and `--prefix-storage cpu` on a multi-MSG cluster_config (two MSGs on one node). Verify a per-node CPU-resident prefix cache is instantiated with block_size 256 and that hits from one MSG benefit the other. Repeat with `--prefix-storage cxl` and verify a single globally shared cache pool is accessed by all MSGs. Confirm hit-rate logs show cross-MSG reuse.
verification.paper_cite: Section V.B (Prefix caching and multi-MSG sharing); Fig. 4(b); Fig. 7(b)

sub_tasks.requirements: Hardware backends and cluster configurations required by the evaluation (Section VI) are supported.

A cluster configuration for the on-premise RTX A6000 server (4× RTX A6000 GPUs, Intel Xeon Gold 6326 CPU) is provided, parameterized with 40 GB device memory, 936 GB/s memory bandwidth, and PCIe 4.0 ×16 interconnect.

verification.spec: Open `cluster_config/` and verify a JSON file exists for the RTX A6000 server. Parse it and assert: num_nodes/topology reflects 4 GPUs, hardware == 'rtx_a6000', npu_mem == 40 GB, memory bandwidth field == 936 GB/s, interconnect == PCIe 4.0 x16, and CPU is configured as Intel Xeon Gold 6326. Load it via main.py with a minimal dataset and verify it parses without error.

sub_tasks.requirements: A cluster configuration for the H100-SXM-80GB cloud server (8× H100, TP=4) is provided, parameterized with 80 GB device memory, 3.35 TB/s memory bandwidth, and NVLink interconnect.

verification.spec: Load the H100 cluster config JSON under cluster_config/ and assert: hardware = H100-SXM-80GB, npu_num=8, npu_mem=80 (GB), memory bandwidth = 3.35 TB/s, interconnect type = NVLink, and parallelism / npu_group reflects tensor parallelism degree 4.

sub_tasks.requirements: A cluster configuration for TPU-v6e-1 is provided, parameterized with 32 GB memory capacity, 1.6 TB/s memory bandwidth, and 800 GB/s interconnect bandwidth.

verification.spec: Load the TPU-v6e-1 cluster config JSON and assert: hardware = TPU-v6e-1, npu_mem=32 (GB), memory bandwidth = 1.6 TB/s, interconnect bandwidth = 800 GB/s.
verification.paper_cite: Section VI, Section VII.C (TPU case study)

sub_tasks.requirements: A cluster configuration for the GPU+PIM system is provided, with a PIM system replacing main memory composed of 256 channels each holding 1 GB of HBM2 at 2000 MT/s, paired with a single RTX A6000 GPU.

verification.spec: Load the GPU+PIM cluster config JSON used in the PIM case study and assert: a single RTX A6000 GPU is paired with a PIM device pool of 256 channels, each channel 1 GB HBM2 at 2000 MT/s. Verify by reading the `pim_config` and `npu_num` fields.

sub_tasks.requirements: Real-system reference deployments used for validation (Section VI) are runnable.
vLLM is set up as the serving framework on RTX A6000 and H100 systems to produce ground-truth throughput, TTFT, TPOT, ITL, memory, and power traces.

verification.spec: Provide scripts/instructions that set up vLLM as the serving framework on RTX A6000 and H100 systems, run Llama 3.1-8B / Llama 3.1-70B / Phi-mini MoE / Mixtral-8x7B with the ShareGPT workload (300 requests, Poisson arrivals at 10 req/s), and collect ground-truth throughput, TTFT, TPOT, ITL, memory usage, and power traces. Verify the scripts execute end-to-end and emit the expected output files used by the validation experiments.

sub_tasks.requirements: TPU-enabled vLLM is set up on a TPU-v6e-1 instance and used to produce ground-truth single-instance dense Llama 3.1-8B serving traces.

verification.spec: Launch vLLM with TPU backend on a TPU-v6e-1 instance serving Llama 3.1-8B with TP=1. Send the ShareGPT-derived request trace and confirm that a throughput/latency log file is produced with per-request TTFT, TPOT, and timestamped token counts.

sub_tasks.requirements: LMCache is integrated with vLLM (block size 256) to provide ground-truth CPU-based prefix caching and centralized KV cache sharing across instances.

verification.spec: Start two vLLM instances configured with LMCache as a centralized CPU-resident prefix cache with block_size=256. Issue a workload containing shared prefixes and verify that LMCache logs show cross-instance prefix hits and that aggregate hit rate exceeds single-instance baseline.

sub_tasks.requirements: Baseline simulators used in the comparison study (Section VII-B) are set up and runnable.
The original LLMServingSim baseline is installed and runnable on supported configurations.

verification.spec: Install the original LLMServingSim from its public release and run its example single-instance dense serving config. Confirm the simulator completes without error and produces runtime statistics output files.

sub_tasks.requirements: Vidur is installed and runnable on supported configurations for comparison.

verification.spec: Install Vidur from its public repo and run a single-instance Llama-style dense config. Confirm the simulator emits throughput/TTFT/TPOT metrics in its output JSON/CSV.

sub_tasks.requirements: APEX is installed and runnable on supported configurations for comparison.

verification.spec: Install APEX from its public repo and run a representative LLM serving configuration; verify it produces latency/throughput predictions for the same model used in cross-simulator comparison.

sub_tasks.requirements: TokenSim is installed and runnable on supported configurations for comparison.

verification.spec: Install TokenSim from its public repo and run a single-instance dense serving scenario; verify it outputs TPS, TTFT, and TPOT metrics.

sub_tasks.requirements: Evaluation metrics computation has been implemented.
Throughput is reported in tokens/sec over time at a configurable `--log-interval`, enabling time-series comparison against real-system traces.

verification.spec: Run a small simulator workload with --log-interval=0.5 and again with --log-interval=2.0. Verify that the throughput log file contains rows at the requested cadence and that the time column is monotonically increasing in tokens/sec units.

sub_tasks.requirements: Per-request latency metrics — TTFT, TPOT, ITL — and their p99 percentiles are computed and aggregated across all requests.

verification.spec: Run the simulator on a small request trace and confirm that the per-request output report contains TTFT, TPOT, and ITL fields for every request, and that aggregate statistics include mean and p99 percentiles for each metric.

sub_tasks.requirements: Memory usage (MB) per device/instance and prefix hit rate (%) are logged over time.

verification.spec: Run the simulator with prefix caching enabled and verify the output log contains a time series of memory_usage_MB per device/instance and prefix_hit_rate_percent columns. Confirm values increase over time as cache fills.

sub_tasks.requirements: Average per-metric percent error between simulator and real system (both time-series and aggregated) is computed via the evaluation/compare.sh utility.

verification.spec: Invoke evaluation/compare.sh with a pair of simulator and real-system CSV files. Verify the script outputs per-metric percent error (mean over time series and aggregated) for throughput, TTFT, TPOT, and ITL.

sub_tasks.requirements: Experiment scripts under evaluation/ (figure_5.sh through figure_10.sh and run_all.sh) execute the full reproduction pipeline.
evaluation/figure_5.sh reproduces the time-series throughput comparison across multi-dense, single-dense + prefix caching, PD-disaggregated, and single-MoE configurations on RTX A6000 and H100.

verification.spec: Execute evaluation/figure_5.sh and verify it produces eight throughput-over-time plots (4 configs x 2 systems: RTX A6000, H100) matching Figure 5. Confirm average per-timestep throughput error vs real-system reference traces is within tolerance (paper reports 5.14% A6000, 3.29% H100).

sub_tasks.requirements: evaluation/figure_6.sh reproduces the RTX A6000 power consumption and energy breakdown experiment under TP1 and TP2, including the request-pulse workload (10 ShareGPT requests issued three times at 60-second intervals with idle gaps).

verification.spec: Execute evaluation/figure_6.sh and verify it produces TP1 and TP2 power-over-time plots and an energy breakdown chart matching Figure 6. Confirm the simulator's total energy error vs the reference RTX A6000 trace is within ~1.34% (paper value) with tolerance 2%.

sub_tasks.requirements: evaluation/figure_7.sh reproduces the memory-usage and prefix-hit-rate comparison on single-instance and multi-instance RTX A6000 deployments with alternating bursty/idle arrivals.

verification.spec: Execute evaluation/figure_7.sh and verify it produces single-instance and multi-instance memory-usage and prefix-hit-rate time-series plots matching Figure 7. Confirm reported average error vs real RTX A6000 traces is within tolerance (paper: 0.93% single, 0.41% multi).

sub_tasks.requirements: evaluation/figure_8.sh reproduces the cross-simulator comparison against LLMServingSim, Vidur, APEX, and TokenSim on accuracy (TPS, TTFT, TPOT) and simulation time across multi-instance, single-instance + prefix caching, PD-disaggregated, and MoE configurations.

verification.spec: Execute evaluation/figure_8.sh and verify it produces accuracy bar charts (TPS, TTFT, TPOT) and simulation-time charts comparing LLMServingSim 2.0 with LLMServingSim, Vidur, APEX, TokenSim, matching Figure 8. Confirm LLMServingSim 2.0 average error is within ~2.43% (single/multi dense) and ~1.81% (PD/MoE).

sub_tasks.requirements: evaluation/figure_9.sh reproduces the TPU-v6e-1 throughput and latency-metric validation on Llama 3.1-8B with TP=1.

verification.spec: Execute evaluation/figure_9.sh and verify it produces TPU-v6e-1 Llama 3.1-8B TP=1 throughput-over-time and aggregated TPS/TPOT/ITL error bars matching Figure 9. Confirm aggregated metric error is below ~0.3% (paper) with tolerance 1%.

sub_tasks.requirements: evaluation/figure_10.sh reproduces the PIM case study comparing GPU-only, GPU+PIM, and GPU+PIM with SBI on Llama 3.1-8B with 256 requests (128 input / 512 output tokens).

verification.spec: Execute evaluation/figure_10.sh and verify it produces throughput-over-time and energy-breakdown plots for GPU-only, GPU+PIM, and GPU+PIM+SBI on Llama 3.1-8B with 256 requests (128 in/512 out). Confirm GPU+PIM throughput speedup over GPU-only is ~1.43x in decode phase and energy-per-token reduction is ~14.8% (tolerance 5%).

sub_tasks.requirements: evaluation/run_all.sh executes the full evaluation pipeline (Figures 5–10) end-to-end within the ~2.5-hour budget reported in the artifact appendix.

verification.spec: Run evaluation/run_all.sh from a clean checkout. Time the wall-clock duration and confirm it completes Figures 5-10 within approximately 2.5 hours, producing all expected output plots and CSV/JSON metric files.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced numerical results match the claims in Section VII (each leaf below should support paper_category="claim", verification.type="measured", paper_value, paper_unit, and a tolerance).
When aggregated across all metrics over the full execution on RTX A6000, the average error is 0.99%.

verification.spec: On RTX A6000, compute end-to-end aggregated metrics (mean throughput, mean TTFT, mean TPOT, mean end-to-end latency) for both real vLLM runs and LLMServingSim 2.0 runs across the four serving scenarios. For each metric compute |sim-real|/real, then average across metrics and scenarios. Verify the resulting mean error is ~0.99%.

sub_tasks.requirements: When aggregated across all metrics over the full execution on H100, the average error is 1.54%.

verification.spec: On H100, compute end-to-end aggregated metrics (throughput, TTFT, TPOT, e2e latency) for both real vLLM runs and LLMServingSim 2.0 across the four scenarios, take mean absolute percentage error across metrics×scenarios. Verify ~1.54%.

sub_tasks.requirements: On the RTX A6000 power experiment, total energy consumption matches the real system with an average error of 1.34% across TP=1 and TP=2.

verification.spec: Reproduce Fig. 6(a)(b): on RTX A6000 server, run 10-request pulses three times at 60 s intervals with idle gaps, at TP=1 and TP=2, measuring real GPU power via nvidia-smi and total system energy. Run LLMServingSim 2.0 with matching config. Compute |E_sim - E_real|/E_real for each TP, then average. Verify ~1.34%.

sub_tasks.requirements: The three-state accelerator power model reproduces the three observed power pulses (active → standby → idle transitions), with higher peak power under TP=2 and shorter duration than TP=1.

verification.spec: Parse the TP1 and TP2 power-over-time logs from evaluation/figure_6.sh and verify three distinct power pulses (corresponding to active->standby->idle transitions) are present in each. Confirm TP=2 peak power exceeds TP=1 peak power and TP=2 pulse duration is shorter than TP=1.

sub_tasks.requirements: The energy breakdown shows accelerators as the dominant consumer, followed by CPU and memory, consistent with the real system.

verification.spec: From the energy breakdown output of evaluation/figure_6.sh, verify that the simulator's accelerator share is the largest among the seven components (excluding 'others'), with CPU and memory as the next-largest, mirroring the real-system breakdown ranking.

sub_tasks.requirements: In the single-instance memory-usage experiment, LLMServingSim 2.0 matches the real system's memory footprint and prefix-cache hit rate with an average error of 0.93%.

verification.spec: Reproduce Fig. 7(a): single instance, GPU prefix caching (vLLM native, block size 16). Sample real-system memory usage and prefix hit rate over time; sample LLMServingSim 2.0 outputs similarly. Compute per-timestep MAPE on both signals, average across the two signals. Verify ~0.93%.

sub_tasks.requirements: In the multi-instance shared-CPU-prefix-cache memory experiment, LLMServingSim 2.0 matches the real system's per-instance memory growth and synchronized hit-rate increases with an average error of 0.41%.

verification.spec: Reproduce Fig. 7(b): two vLLM instances with LMCache-backed shared CPU prefix cache (block size 256). Record per-instance device memory and aggregate prefix-cache hit rate over time on both real system and LLMServingSim 2.0. Compute per-timestep MAPE averaged across signals; verify ~0.41%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Overall, across all reported metrics, LLMServingSim 2.0 achieves an average error of 0.95% versus the real serving deployments.

verification.spec: Aggregate the per-metric errors reported in Section VII-A (throughput, TTFT, TPOT, memory, prefix hit rate, power) across all validation experiments (RTX A6000 + H100), and compute the overall arithmetic mean. Verify the mean is ~0.95%.

sub_tasks.requirements: End-to-end simulation time stays around 10 minutes for the complex multi-instance, prefix-caching, PD-disaggregated, and MoE configurations.

verification.spec: Run LLMServingSim 2.0 on the complex configurations (multi-instance, prefix-caching, PD-disaggregated, MoE) used in Section VII-B Fig. 8(b). Measure wall-clock simulation time for each. Verify the mean wall-clock is ~10 minutes (allow 5–20 min range).

sub_tasks.requirements: In Figure 8(a), LLMServingSim 2.0 achieves an average error of 2.43% across TPS/TTFT/TPOT under single- and multi-instance dense serving configurations.

verification.spec: Reproduce Fig. 8(a) for single- and multi-instance dense serving: run vLLM (reference) and LLMServingSim 2.0; compute |sim/real - 1| for TPS, TTFT, and TPOT per scenario and average across all three metrics and both scenarios. Verify ~2.43%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Under PD-disaggregation and MoE configurations, LLMServingSim 2.0 achieves an average error of 1.81% across supported metrics, whereas several baselines fail to execute or rely on simplified abstractions.

verification.spec: Reproduce Fig. 8(a) for PD-disaggregation and MoE configurations: compute the mean |sim/real - 1| across TPS, TTFT, TPOT averaged over both configurations for LLMServingSim 2.0. Verify ~1.81%. Additionally verify that Vidur/APEX/TokenSim/original LLMServingSim either fail to run or produce errors > LLMServingSim 2.0 on these configurations.

sub_tasks.requirements: Compared to the original LLMServingSim, LLMServingSim 2.0 substantially reduces simulation time while expanding the supported design space, as shown in Figure 8(b).

verification.spec: From evaluation/figure_8.sh simulation-time results, confirm that LLMServingSim 2.0's wall-clock simulation time is strictly lower than the original LLMServingSim's on the configurations both support, while LLMServingSim 2.0 additionally produces non-error results on PD-disaggregated and MoE configurations that the original cannot run.

sub_tasks.requirements: On the TPU-v6e-1 single-instance dense Llama 3.1-8B experiment, average per-timestep throughput error is 3.95%.

verification.spec: On TPU-v6e-1 with vLLM-TPU serving Llama 3.1-8B (TP=1, ShareGPT 300 req, Poisson 10 req/s), record real-system per-timestep throughput. Run LLMServingSim 2.0 with TPU profile and matched memory/bandwidth (32 GB, 1.6 TB/s, 800 GB/s ICI). Compute per-timestep MAPE between simulator and real throughput curves. Verify ~3.95%.

sub_tasks.requirements: When aggregated over the full TPU run, the average error across throughput, TTFT, TPOT, and ITL drops below 0.3%.

verification.spec: On the same TPU experiment, compute aggregate (mean over full run) values for throughput (TPS), TPOT, and ITL on both real vLLM-TPU and LLMServingSim 2.0. Compute |sim/real - 1| per metric; verify that all three are below 0.3% and that the geometric mean is approximately 0.296%.

sub_tasks.requirements: The PIM case study (Figure 10) is reproduced and matches the paper's conclusions.
During the prefill-dominated period (0–25 s), GPU+PIM shows limited speedup vs GPU-only.

verification.spec: From the PIM case-study throughput trace (Fig. 10(a)), restrict to t in [0, 25] s (prefill-dominated period). Verify that GPU+PIM throughput is within ~10% of GPU-only throughput in this window (i.e., no significant speedup), confirming PIM benefits are limited during prefill.

sub_tasks.requirements: After prefill completes, GPU+PIM achieves 1.43× higher throughput than the GPU-only system.

verification.spec: Configure LLMServingSim 2.0 per Section VII-C PIM case study: Llama 3.1-8B, GPU-only on RTX A6000 vs. GPU+PIM (256 channels × 1 GB HBM2 @ 2000 MT/s), workload of 256 requests with 128 input / 512 output tokens. Run both setups; compute steady-state (post-prefill, e.g., t ≥ 25 s) mean token-generation throughput. Verify ratio GPU+PIM / GPU-only ≈ 1.43×.

sub_tasks.requirements: GPU+PIM with SBI achieves performance comparable to GPU-only on this workload because small effective per-GPU batches limit SBI benefits; SBI becomes effective only when very large batch sizes (≥256) are sustained (e.g. in the 26–35 s interval).

verification.spec: From the PIM case-study throughput trace, verify (a) over the full run GPU+PIM-with-SBI throughput is within ~10% of GPU-only (comparable), and (b) within the 26–35 s window where effective batch size reaches/exceeds 256, GPU+PIM-with-SBI exceeds GPU-only throughput by a measurable margin. Confirm the explanation that SBI benefits only manifest at sustained batch sizes ≥ 256.

sub_tasks.requirements: GPU+PIM achieves the lowest energy consumption among the three setups, reducing Joules per token by 14.8% versus GPU-only.

verification.spec: Using the same PIM case-study run, compute Joules-per-token = total simulated energy / total generated tokens for both GPU-only and GPU+PIM. Verify GPU+PIM reduces J/token by ~14.8% relative to GPU-only, and that GPU+PIM has the lowest J/token among the three setups (GPU-only, GPU+PIM, GPU+PIM w/ SBI).

sub_tasks.requirements: GPU+PIM with SBI consumes more energy than GPU-only due to additional PIM activity without matching throughput gains.

verification.spec: Run the GPU-only, GPU+PIM, and GPU+PIM+SBI configurations from the PIM case study (Llama 3.1-8B, RTX A6000 + 256-channel PIM, 256 ShareGPT-style requests with 128/512 in/out tokens). Assert: (a) total throughput of GPU+PIM+SBI is comparable to GPU-only (within ~10%) and lower than GPU+PIM, and (b) total energy of GPU+PIM+SBI > total energy of GPU-only and > total energy of GPU+PIM. Verify by reading the simulator energy CSV output.

sub_tasks.requirements: Ablation studies from the paper

Code has been implemented and run such that GPU+PIM achieves approximately 1.43x higher decode-phase throughput than GPU-only on Llama 3.1-8B with 256 requests (input=128, output=512), as in Figure 10(a).

verification.spec: Run simulate_pim_case_study(model='llama3.1-8b', num_reqs=256, in_toks=128, out_toks=512); compute ratio = tps_gpu_pim_decode / tps_gpu_only_decode; assert |ratio - 1.43| / 1.43 <= 0.20

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that GPU+PIM reduces Joules-per-token by approximately 14.8% compared to GPU-only on Llama 3.1-8B with 256 requests, as in Figure 10(b).

verification.spec: Run simulate_pim_energy(model='llama3.1-8b', num_reqs=256); compute reduction_pct = (j_per_tok_gpu_only - j_per_tok_gpu_pim) / j_per_tok_gpu_only * 100; assert |reduction_pct - 14.8| / 14.8 <= 0.30; also produce per-component breakdown (GPU, CPU, memory, link, NIC, storage, others) for all three configs (GPU-only, GPU+PIM, GPU+PIM+SBI)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that simulated total energy consumption under TP=1 and TP=2 on the RTX A6000 system both match the real-system measurements with average error around 1.34%, as in Figure 6(a)(b).

verification.spec: Run simulate_power(model='llama3.1-8b', gpu='rtx-a6000', tp_degree=[1,2], pulses=3, pulse_interval_s=60, requests_per_pulse=10); for each TP, compute energy_error = |E_sim - E_real| / E_real; assert mean(energy_error over TP1, TP2) <= 0.05 (paper reports 1.34%); also assert TP=2 peak power > TP=1 peak power

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that single-instance memory usage error is approximately 0.93% and multi-instance memory/prefix-hit-rate error is approximately 0.41%, validating prefix cache sharing modeling as in Figure 7.

verification.spec: Run simulate_prefix_memory(setup='single') and simulate_prefix_memory(setup='multi'); for each, compute mean(|sim - real| / real) over memory-usage and hit-rate time-series; assert single_err <= 0.05 and multi_err <= 0.05

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that prefix-caching ablations covering (a) disabled, (b) device-only, (c) CPU shared, and (d) CXL shared tiers are all executable in the simulator and report distinct memory usage and prefix hit-rate behaviors.

verification.spec: Run simulate_prefix_tiers(modes=['off','device','cpu_shared','cxl_shared']); assert all 4 modes complete; assert hit_rate('off') == 0; assert hit_rate('cpu_shared') >= hit_rate('device'); assert hit_rate('cxl_shared') >= hit_rate('cpu_shared') OR within 5%; assert device memory footprint differs across tiers

sub_tasks.requirements: Method components (from critique splits and additions)
Online runtime statistics (memory usage, energy, throughput) are emitted at a configurable interval controlled by --log-interval.

Load the per-request output CSV (or JSON) with pandas; assert the column set contains case-insensitive matches for {ttft, tpot, itl, queue (or queueing_delay), e2e (or end_to_end_latency)} and that the file has exactly 8 data rows.

verification.paper_cite: Section IV-A Outputs; Section VII-A latency metrics

sub_tasks.requirements: The one-time initialization sequence (Execution Planner building Serving Engine, MSGs, Router, System Simulator) executes exactly once before the runtime loop begins.

verification.spec: Monkey-patch (or instrument via unittest.mock) the ExecutionPlanner.initialize / __init__ entry points (e.g., `ExecutionPlanner.build_serving_engine`) and MSG constructor; run a short end-to-end simulation. Assert that `build_serving_engine` is called exactly once, MSG constructors are called exactly once per configured model, and that the first runtime-loop iteration starts only AFTER all initialization calls have returned (verified via call-order timestamps).
verification.paper_cite: Section IV-A Serving workflow; Section IV-B

sub_tasks.requirements: Each runtime-loop iteration dispatches requests through the pipeline Router → MSG → graph generator → System Simulator.

verification.spec: Instrument RequestRouter.dispatch, MSG.schedule_batch (or equivalent), GraphGenerator.build, and SystemSimulator.run with mock spies recording call timestamps. Run a simulation of >=4 requests. Assert that within each iteration the call order is Router < MSG < GraphGenerator < SystemSimulator, and that this ordered tuple appears at least once per request.

sub_tasks.requirements: Simulation terminates only after every request has a recorded completion timestamp, and simulated time advances monotonically throughout the run.

verification.spec: Run a simulation with N=16 requests. After completion: (a) load the per-request output and assert every request has a non-null completion/finish timestamp and finish >= arrival; (b) collect the sequence of simulated 'current time' values logged during the runtime loop (instrument the loop or read time-stamped log entries) and assert the sequence is monotonically non-decreasing; (c) assert max(finish_time) <= final simulated time reported in summary.
